relevant_files.role: Main security scanner implementation using PatternEngine
Embedded pattern database for security rules
Performance benchmarks for current regex-based scanner
Integration tests including multi-line limitation test
Project overview and security scanning features

conventions.commits: Conventional commits with signed commits and DCO sign-off
conventions.testing: Unit tests in same file as implementation, integration tests in tests/ directory, negative tests for known limitations
conventions.linting: Clippy pedantic lints with some allowances (module_name_repetitions, missing_errors_doc, missing_panics_doc), rustfmt for formatting
conventions.error_handling: thiserror and anyhow for error handling, tracing for logging
conventions.import/module_organization: Clear module separation with security/ submodule containing scanner, patterns, types, etc.

patterns: Regex-based pattern matching with file extension filtering
Lazy static initialization for global PatternEngine
Hybrid diff/file scanning with ignore rules
Negative testing for known limitations (multi-line detection)

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Documents the architectural limitation that tree-sitter aims to solve

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Implements the negative test for multi-line limitation

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection

constraints: Current scanner operates line-by-line, cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities where source and sink are on different lines
Performance critical: benchmarks show <10ms for 500-line scans
No external service scanning: all processing must be local
Must support multiple languages (Rust, Python, JavaScript, etc.)
test_coverage: High for regex-based patterns: includes integration tests with vulnerable and safe fixtures, negative test for multi-line limitation, and benchmarks. Security scanner has 14+ unit tests covering various scenarios including file scanning, diff scanning, multiple files, line numbers, and ignore rules.

library_findings.relevant_api: Parser setup with language grammars, parse() method, query API with S-expression queries, incremental parsing, syntax tree navigation
library_findings.notes: Core incremental parsing library. Requires language-specific grammars. Rust API is mature with good documentation and examples. Query system uses S-expressions for pattern matching.

library_findings.relevant_api: LANGUAGE constant for parser configuration, query files for syntax highlighting, node type information
library_findings.notes: Rust grammar for tree-sitter. Provides LANGUAGE constant for Rust parsing. Includes query files that can be adapted for security scanning. Well-maintained and widely used.

library_findings.relevant_api: Query::new(), QueryCursor::exec(), capture system for pattern matching
library_findings.notes: Tree-sitter's query language (S-expressions) for AST pattern matching. Enables language-aware pattern matching beyond simple regex. Can capture specific nodes like function calls, assignments, etc.

approaches.name: Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter Scanner
approaches.description: Maintain existing regex patterns for simple single-line detections while adding tree-sitter-based scanning for multi-line and complex patterns. Use tree-sitter queries to detect data-flow patterns like taint tracking from source to sink across multiple lines.
pros: Can leverage existing ignore rules and configuration
Incremental parsing for fast re-scans on edits
cons: Learning curve for tree-sitter query syntax
Binary size increases from grammar crates
Maintenance overhead for multiple language grammars

approaches.name: Full Tree-Sitter Migration
approaches.description: Replace regex-based PatternEngine entirely with tree-sitter-based scanning. Rewrite all patterns as tree-sitter queries. Implement taint tracking and data-flow analysis using AST traversal and query results.
pros: Better context for findings (exact variable names, function calls)
Supports complex vulnerability patterns (taint tracking, control flow)
Enables advanced features like call graph analysis
cons: Major architecture change (500-800 new lines estimated)
Performance impact from AST parsing (though incremental helps)
All existing patterns need rewriting as queries

approaches.name: Tree-Sitter Spike for Multi-Line Detection Only
approaches.description: Implement a minimal spike: add tree-sitter-rust to the project, create a proof-of-concept that can detect multi-line SQL injection (source variable on one line, query execution on another), then evaluate complexity, performance, and detection quality before deciding on full implementation.
pros: Validates tree-sitter approach before full implementation
Provides concrete data for decision making
Can reuse spike code if approach is approved
cons: Spike code may need to be thrown away
Doesn't solve the full problem immediately
Time spent may not lead to adoption

recommendation: Start with Approach 3 (Tree-Sitter Spike) to validate the approach with minimal risk. The spike should focus on detecting multi-line SQL injection in Rust code using tree-sitter-rust. This will provide concrete performance metrics, detection accuracy data, and team learning to make an informed decision about Approach 1 (Hybrid) vs Approach 2 (Full Migration). The spike can be done in a separate branch and the findings used to drive the architectural decision for issue #737.
